title: 2026/03/17 ENV (Part 1)
description: Tuesday, 17 March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Catchment Communities Aotearoa Ben Ensor, Chair Stuart MacIntyre, General Manager Hawkes Bay Regional Planning Committee Councillor Hinewai Ormsby, Co-chairs Tania Hopmans, Co-chairs Water New Zealand Gillian Blythe, Chief Executive Helen Atkins, Barrister and former Past President of Water New Zealand Urban Design Forum Ekin Sakin, Jade McFarlane, Graeme Scott, Eva Zombori, Parininihi ki Waitotara Sarah Mako, Alisha Ross New Zealand Sport Fishing Council Inc. Rowan Ashton, Special Counsel Hobson's pledge Dr Don Brash, Mr Stephen Franks, Mr Max Pakinga-Barber, Te Runanganui o Ngati Porou Keita Kohere, Chief Advisor Te Tai Kaha Kingi Smiler, Chair Liz Mellish, Deputy Chair, Federation of Māori Authorities Dr Mahina-a-rangi Baker, Technical Expert for Te Tai Kaha (Iwi Planner) Dr Richard Meade, Technical Expert for Te Tai Kaha (Economist) Anne Carter, Te Tai Kaha Māori Collective Secretariat Papa Pounamu Dion Luke, Co-Chair New Zealand Freshwater Sciences Society Dr Joanne Clapcott, President Dr Richard Allibone, Vice President Dr Jennifer Price, Submissions Coordinator Geoscience Society of New Zealand Bruce Hayward, Geoheritage Subcommittee convenor
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Catchment Communities Aotearoa

chunks.text: And I can see Ben there and Stuart as well. Welcome to the select committee this morning. You have twenty minutes with us, so take your submission as read. But if you'd like to, kick off by talking us through the main points, and then we can open up for questions.

So we'll hand it over to you. Oh, Ben, you're just on mute there. You just got to, turn on your audio. We'll try that one. There we go. Good morning all. We can hear you. Good morning. Morning Catherine, and thank you for the opportunity to be here this morning. So yes, my name is Ben Itzla. I'm the Chair of Catchment Communities Aotearoa. Uh, so a little bit on me.

I'm a sheep and beef farmer from the Hurunui and, as well as being involved with catchment groups for the last ten years, have been involved with, in the ECAN Zone Committee process. So, coming up with community lead solutions leading into, the old RMA f- flip, planning framework, so some experience there.

Um, but yes, so Catchment Communities Aotearoa is a membership organization representing approximately two hundred and twenty catchment groups across the country. Uh, and those groups covering some twelve million hectares of, of farmed land, in, in New Zealand. So, I guess the, just a bit of background, the evolution of catchment groups is quite interesting, just how they're evolving right now from what started as mainly smaller groups with single issues, in a catchment addressing water quality mainly. They are now,

amalgamating, you might say, into coming under bigger umbrella organizations, some covering, whole provinces. So Thriving Southland and Taranaki Catchment Collective would be two good examples where there is an umbrella organization with still the smaller, local connected catchment groups underneath them.

Um, and this is a really good model because it maintains the grassroots connection, which is so important for, the action these groups takes, but also gives them a bit more of a structure and some support around, the administration side of things. But, also, gives them the ability to interact with, planning processes and other organizations where it's, where it's applicable.

So, and I guess, yeah, these groups are also really looking at the whole range of environmental factors now. So not just water quality, but a huge amount of work on biodiversity, as, as well, and together with community work, bringing communities together and building resilience. Um, look, so the real essence of our submission is to ask that a community lead approaches and collective approaches, are not only part of these two new bills, but, are, are pri-prioritized in the working of them in terms of coming up with solutions or coming up with how we're going to,

to address the environmental issues that we need to. Um, so that's not to say we don't need a regulatory framework. We absolutely do. But we are asking that, that the community approach, the collective approach is given priority at the front end of these processes to give the community an opportunity to come together and create their own solution and therefore own it, because that is so much more powerful than just relying on rules alone to make behavior change. So areas where this approach is applicable is obviously in the development of, natural environment

plans. Um, giving the community the ability to feed into those, whether it's through catchment groups or other organizations, empowers them, to understand why and, and a- and develop actions that can address the issues across a range, a whole range of factors.

Um, obviously the Section sixty, sixty-five talks about action plans. I mean, this is really key and we really like the way and wanna, wanna promote th- th- this concept, but also really, emphasize, the need, for the community input into that through catchment groups and those type of organizations.

Um, but, and, and also make the point that this mechanism should be used even in areas where there is real environmental pressure or even over allocation, because giving the, the opportunity to the community first to come up with a solution is more powerful. Obviously, if they can't come up with that solution or it's not deemed to be an effective solution, well then it has to go back to a regulator to come up with a, with another way forward. But, giving that first opportunity to, to community groups, catchment groups,

is a way to bring the community with you and get them, involved and on side. Um, obviously setting of limits, environmental limits, fully accept that, national health limits are, you know, need to be set at a national level. But there needs to be regional or local input into the setting of environmental limits to make them appropriate to the, the natural environment, the geography, the land use, and, and, and the local people's expectation, making them achievable, and, a-and having reasonable time frames to achieve that outcome and to that. Um,

so I guess, I think there's a real opportunity here for, the same approach. I mean, biodiversity is a really good example of how this, a more collective, a more community lead approach can be very effective. I mean, biodiversity doesn't sit within catchments, and catchment groups, community approaches have the ability to address it across a whole area, across, in a, in a much more larger scale. Um, you know, we need to think of corridors for biodiversity, so if groups of, um-Catchment groups, groups of landowners, communities can come

together and propose management plans to address their biodiversity issues, then, they should be given weight, because, you know, compared to a more protectionist approach we've tried to take in the past, which is essentially, you know, putting a fence around areas and, and, and, and h- hoping that that does the job.

We know that, we need to manage good outcomes in terms of weeds, pests, if we're gonna get the outcomes we need. So you need people to do the management, and you need them involved, whereas a straight rules-based approach doesn't foster that and it often actually makes that biodiversity, more of a liability, than an asset in the eyes of the landowner, which is, is detrimental.

Um, all these things, farm plans are a really key part of, how you interact with your community, how you interact with landowners. So we'd ask that, that the catchment collectives are recognized as the, a, the, a good place, the best place to start with rolling out farm plans.

Many catch farmers, landowners already have a farm plan developed through a catchment group and have interacted with them to do that. So it's the logical place they will go back to in the future to, to satisfy any, additional requirements of, of, of the new f- for, certified freshwater farm plans.

Uh, so we'd like to see that. I mean, there's not a lot of detail in, in how the farm plans will exact- will roll out and be, implemented. So bringing, using catchment collectives as part of that is, is a really key, for us. Um, so I mean, yeah, that's...

A- and also final point, and is just to really reiterate the comments from, Simon Upton, Commissioner for the Environment, around the standardizing of protocols and methodology for monitoring the environment. Um, there's a huge range of different tests and different methodologies used across the country. It makes it very difficult to, to quantify how we're doing. Um, and it-- if we could have standard protocols and standard, methodologies for monitoring the environment, it would also allow a lot of other groups...

I mean, some catchment groups are doing a huge amount of water testing, for example, across the country. But i- in some instances, it's not clear h- how, relevant that work will be given it doesn't match with, or there is not a standard that it, a, a, a, a, a uniform standard across the country where it can be used.

So if that was standardized and agreed, then, it would bring a lot more information to the table, as well as allowing a lot more comparison across the country, which would obviously be very useful. Um, I'd think our final point is just with, any collaborative process, any community process, it needs, that process needs to be given more weight through the, as it, well, what we used to call the RMA process. But as it moves forward, one of the most disempowering things is for a community to come together and thrash out

some, what are some really difficult conversations about allocation, about, you know, land use. These are tough con- conversations. But then if it's not respected as it goes forward, and, those with big budgets from outside the district can come in and unduly influence that plan and change it materially, then that completely disempowers the people who put it together, who put their, um their heart and their soul and their time into coming up with a solution for themselves to have it then transformed into something that's quite different,

is, is, is incredibly disengaging. And that has happened many times under the old system. Um, and I've been involved in those processes. So if we're going to ask communities to come together to form solutions, we need to give those solutions weight and, and, and let them be taken through. Um, obviously, they still need to be accountable, and that's the council's job, to monitor the environment and make sure those plans are effective.

And there will be another planning, solution, process down the track, and that may need to change. But we need to respect those, plans in the first instance. So look, I'll leave it there. And obviously, yeah, I'm happy to answer questions on any of that. And, yeah. Look, thank you, Ben, for that oversight and for your submission. Uh, a really interesting area, obviously, catchment groups and the involvement and the amazing work that you do across the country.

Look, we've got a couple of questions. We'll go to Rachel Brooking first. Oh, look, I'd say I, I would... So one step back. So k- the requirements need to be commensurate to the environmental pressure in that, that catchment. So if I was holding the pen, particularly in, lower risk catchments, then a, a, a farm plan developed as part of a catchment group, th- that would negate the need for a certifier, is the wording

currently. Um, and there can be ways of having checks and balances in that process to give everyone, certainty that those farmers are, are, you know, actually taking the process seriously without auditing every farmer, because that's a significant cost in an area where, some areas where there is li- little envi- you know, not as much environmental pressure. So that's one thing.

Um-And more and higher... I mean, there's some really good examples in Canterbury about, much more onerous, audited self-management system where, there's agreements with the regulator in terms of what's in those plans and what needs to happen, and are audited on a more, stringent basis. So, but it's allowing the catchment community, the catchment group to be part of developing those plans, which really gives good, catchment context and rolling them out, obviously with appropriate checks and balances based on the environmental pressure in the area.

So that's, quickly how I would describe it. And just quickly there, so, would your focus then... So you just talked about the development of the plans, but also that the audit would be sort of via the catchment group process rather than farm by f- farm in a low risk environment. In a low risk area, yes. You, there's two ways you could do it.

You could audit the catchment group process, which would involve looking at some plans, or you could order a sub set- set of farmers on a, you know, an annual basis, just, you know... So there's a number of ways you could structure something that's not asking everyone to go through that process regularly to give, assurance that that catchment group is, is taking the, you know, doing it properly.

Thank you. Look, Ryan Hamilton has a question. Thanks. Uh, just to follow on, on the, freshwater plans. Uh, we had some submissions yesterday talking about they were keen to have freshwater plans replace resource consents. I don't think we're gonna get there, you know, like for like on everything, but what's your view around the intersection of freshwater plans, resource consents?

Oh, look, there's certainly a place where that is absolutely applicable and, and some, like, I mean, okay, winter grazing would be an example of, of a good area that if there's a good winter plan, or, sorry, a good farm plan in place that, that talks well to how the winter grazing is managed, but then, then that a really good example of why then you shouldn't need a, a consent as well.

But when you, when you go through to, consents to take water, for example, I can't see how that can really fit into a, a farm planning scenario. So a bit of a horses for courses, but yes, in terms of, yeah, winter grazing, biodiversity management, a lot of, more management type scenarios, then absolutely.

Um, but it is a case by case basis. Yeah, no, thank you. And just on, in your submission, a key component that stood out was lack of a formal recognition for catchment groups and, or, you know, w- you weren't, I guess, captured by interested parties. So do you think that needs to be in this primary legislation, or are you, would you be happy if it came through in the next phase with the national direction and, and the policy?

Look, I think th- they do need to be recognized, and that's what we asked for initially. So it's about inserting it in the appropriate place. Um, it's key to realize that our bigger, give, give the catchment groups the option and recognize them formally for a start, I think is really important. Not all catchment groups have the capacity to be fully involved in a planning process now. But, I believe going forward, that will become more prominent.

Um, and, if those that take up the option will be very valuable to be in those. So I, I, I think that would be... I mean, I'm not a, I don't write plans, but, recognizing them in the process is paramount for them to be effective going forward. Thank you. Just a quick sup on that. Do most farmers, have their catchment groups involved in their freshwater farm plans? Is that, is that a standard practice across New Zealand?

It is very, very common. Um, so I mean, in the more, I guess, where there's more environmental pressure, then, farm plans have been mandated and it's been all, you know, different consultants and a number of different organizations who have rolled out farm plans and obviously supported by industry bodies. But for huge areas of the country, the, the f- the, catchment group has been the primary vehicle for rolling out farm plans because they've been voluntary, and they're obviously an incredibly good tool for farmers to understand their environment and the actions they can take.

So I would say, I don't have numbers on it, but I would say the majority of farm plans in the country have been rolled out with support from catchment groups, the by clear majority. So naturally, those farmers will then go back to the catchment group. Mm. Whenever new regulations or whenever new requirements for those farm plans come about, the first person they will ring or get in contact with is the catchment group who they trust and who helped them set that up in the first place.

Oh, good. Good. Um, okay. Grant McCallum. Yeah. Good morning, Ben. Um, my question is, yes, Dairy NZ presented yesterday, and, amongst their submission, they highlight the fact that measuring the out- the impact of on, on farming from, on a catchment scale is quite accurate, versus farm by farm is less accurate, and that's been shown to be the case. How do you address that in a catchment situation? 'Cause I mean, you know, y- you've got a wide range of people.

How do you, how do you approach that challenge? So agree with their statement. Um, how is it addressed? Um, catchment groups are about taking, you know, the, the working with the willing, taking people with you. But what happens is you take the vast majority because of the social way they work. So and that is a good outcome. If we get n- 95% of people on board doing the right thing, then progress is made.

Um, the reality is, and it's not just farming, any, any, industry, any profession, there's a small percentage who may be a bit slower for whatever reason, but that's the job of the regulator. The catchment groups take the-Vast majority move them forward. And so measuring on a catchment scale is definitely more accurate than at a farm scale. Um, and so if you empower that community process, that ev- helps get more people on board and helps...

I mean, that's what, you know, catchment groups are about, tools not rules. They're about, they come together to get stuff done. So, I said at the start, we need an environment, a regulatory framework, but if we empower the community-led catchment group approach, we're gonna get so much more done. Yeah. Thanks. That's very, very helpful.

Thank you. Look, I might just jump in with one last question. Um, you mentioned here that you had concern, that poorly collaborated limits or market tools risk sidelining community-led work. Mm. What, can you just kind of clarify what you mean there? Yeah. I- Maybe give a practical example.

Oh, well, I guess we're not seeing a lot of resource allocation through commercial mechanisms, currently, but that would potentially cut across community-led solutions, 'cause community-led solutions aren't just about, financial return. And if we went to a market-led approach for, fully market-led approach for allocating resource, it would go to the, the highest paying land use. Now, that may not always be the best environmental outcome. So that's our real concern, that if, if it, on a... From what we hear, that's not the

intention, but you've gotta be really careful what goes in a plan becomes the plan, and that's what it is. So, yeah, look, so there is concern that if a fully market-led approach doesn't actually consider the environmental outcome, and we need a more holistic, way to consider our environmental limits and our allocation rather than simply market-led.

Look, thank you very much, Ben and Stuart. We have met time now. Uh, we appreciate your time and all the work that you're doing across the country with the catchment groups, really important work, and a, and a really good submission, some valuable considerations there. So thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you.

Right. So we are now moving on to our next submission today, the Hawke's Bay Regional Planning Committee. And we can see you there, Tania, and I think Hinewai, is popping up there as well. Um, good morning. Good morning to you both. Uh, welcome to the select committee this morning.

You have 20 minutes with us. Um, of course, take your submission as read, but we'll hand over to you to talk us through the main points, and then we'll open up for some questions. So over to you. Kia ora, Catherine. Um, can you hear me okay? Yes. Yep, we can hear you. Yeah, fantastic. Very good. Well, tena koe te tiamana, Catherine.

Tena koutou, e te committee. So thank you, Chair, and members of the committee for the opportunity to speak today on this important kaupapa. So the proposed bills and their practical impacts on the Hawke's Bay Regional Planning Committee. So in these submissions, we will refer to the Hawke's Bay Regional Planning Committee as RPC for short, and Post-Settlement Governance Entities as PSGEs. Uh, my name is Hinewai Ormsby, and I appear before you as the co-chair of the Hawke's Bay RPC, alongside my colleague and co-chair, Tania

Hopmans. I'm currently serving my third term as a regional councilor, and in the previous term had the privilege of being the chair of the Hawke's Bay Regional Council. Uh, Tania helped to negotiate the treaty settlement redress, which resulted in the establishment of the RPC in 2011. So she has been a member of the RPC since it was formed 15 years ago.

So this morning, with the committee, we will focus briefly on a few of the practical issues for the RPC arising from the bills, leaving time for questions for the members of the committee at the end. Um, so just to recap, the RPC is a co-governance body established under its own legislation.

The RPC at the, is the result of treaty settlements with nine groups of hapū and iwi throughout Hawke's Bay, and it brings together the Hawke's Bay Regional Council and the nine PSGEs to make regional planning decisions together, including the oversight of regional policy statement and regional plans. So our submission focuses on one central point. The bills must recognize and carry forward the Hawke's Bay RPC into the new planning system.

So specifically, this means, first, the RPC must be explicitly recognized in legislation. Second, the RPC must retain the same status and equivalent responsibilities as it has now, and this translates into the RPC having a decision-making role in developing the regional spatial plan and the, and the natural environment plan from the time that the bills are enacted. And the third is the bills must include treaty-related obligations at least equivalent to or better than those under the RMA.

And failure to do these things, will be a direct and a egregious, a egregious, breach of treaty, existing treaty settlements with Hawke's Bay hapū and iwi. It will be a breach of Te Tiriti and a breach of the principles of the treaty and inconsistent with the commitments of Clause 10 of the bills. So put simply, the machinery for treaty partnership in regional planning already exists in Hawke's Bay. So the reform must build on this foundation, not delay or minimize it.The structure and operation of the

RPC is well understood within the Hawke's Bay Regional Council, and it will be relatively simple to ensure the same structure is provided for in the new system by replacing the role of the RPC in the regional policy statement and regional planning processes with the equivalent function in the regional spatial plan and natural environment plan. So providing this degree of certainty at this stage in the process will avoid any future confusion and debate over how the RPC should operate in the new system or what the effect of

clause 10 in relation to the RPC will be. Um, so that's, that's my part, and I'll hand over to my colleague, Tania Hopmans. Thank you. Atamari akoato. Can you hear me okay? Ka pai. Yes. Um, I'm going... Thank you. I'm gonna talk briefly about spatial planning in the RPC. So in our written submissions, we gave detailed reasons for why the RP- RPC should be responsible for preparing the regional spatial plan. Uh, but we thought it would also be helpful to provide some additional context about why the RPC was established as Treaty Settlement Redress.

Um, in short, all of the treaty claims of the nine hapū and iwi groups in Hawke's Bay involve significant treaty breaches by The Crown in relation to natural resources, such as land, sites of significance, rivers, lakes, wetlands, the coastal marine area, and flora and fauna, as well as the impacts of mismanagement by The Crown, which resulted in sedimentation, flooding, and other hazards. So one form of redress that was offered was membership of a committee that developed regional documents of the highest order.

That is, at that time, they were the regional policy statement, the regional resource management plan, and the coastal environment plan. Participating as decision-makers at a regional level enabled PSGEs, representing those groups, to influence the policies and the rules that impacted the natural resources in the area. Now, the government proposals include the development of a racial s- a regional spatial, plan. Uh, regional spatial plans will be critical for shaping the future of our region in a takiwā of each hapū and, those iwi

groups. Regional spatial plans will identify where growth occurs, where infrastructure is built, and how environmental limits are managed. These are precisely the types of decisions the RPC already oversees through the de- through the development of the regional policy statement. Therefore, it's logical for the RPC structure to form the basis of the spatial planning committee for the Hawke's Bay region. Amendments could be made to the council membership to provide for the territorial authorities to be included alongside the HBRC, the Hawke's Bay Regional

Council, while also maintaining the co-governance model that already exists with the PSGEs. In many respects, Hawke's Bay has already been piloting the type of integrated regional planning this reform seeks to achieve nationally and has an appropriate governance structure already in place through the RPC. Adapting this already operational model will allow Hawke's Bay to transition quickly into the new planning framework, while importantly ensuring that treaty settlements are upheld.

I'd like to turn now briefly to transitional risk. The current drafting of the bills creates potentially significant risks, not only to the government, but also the council and PSGEs, due to the proposed timing of the transition to the new system. Under Clause 9 of the bills, the couns- The Crown will enter, agreements to uphold Treaty Settlement Redress within two years of the bills being enacted, while under Schedule 1, regional spatial plans must be notified within 15 months. This creates a real risk that regional spatial plans are developed and notified

before The Crown has reached agreement with PSGEs about how the, how the RPC is transitioned into the new system. Strategic planning decisions that shape land use, infrastructure, and environmental limits for generations could begin before governance arrangements are settled, and this is unworkable and unacceptable.

Planning should not run ahead of confirming governance arrangements. Um, and so we'd, we'd like to put forward and re-emphasize our recommendations, and our submission proposes three practical solutions. Uh, the first is explicitly recognize the RPC in legislation. Secondly, to provide the RPC with an equivalent governance role for the development of the regional spatial plan and natural environment plan. And thirdly, that the bills must include treaty-related obligations at least equivalent to or better

than those in the RMA, especially in Section 6E, 7A, and 8. Uh, so we note that we haven't elaborated on, on this third, recommendation in our verbal submissions, because we consider it well covered by other submitters, including Tina Porour and Ricky Allison. Um, so in closing, on behalf of Tania and I as co-chairs of the RPC, Hawke's Bay has already demonstrated that treaty partnership and regional planning can work effectively, and it has been.

Uh, councils and iwi across the region recognize the value of collaborative spatial planning done well. And the Regional Planning Committee provides a well understood and effective structure where future planning decis- decisions can be made together, for the long-term future of our region. Um, We're happy to take questions now. Thank you, Catherine.

Yeah, Kia ora. Thank you, Hinewai and Tania. Uh, look, really interesting points, being made, and thank you for your submission. Um, look, we've got a couple of questions, so we'll go to Huhana first. Uh, tēnā koe Hinewaioti, rā Tania. Yeah. Uh, e whitu mai ki waenganui a mātou tenei ata.

Um, warm greetings to you both, and thank you for coming with very, clear recommendations to the committee. I wanted to invite and just understand, in the drafting of this legislation, have officials spent any time with your committee to learn around the role and function and opportunities to capture the essence of what you've been doing for many years in this legislation? Have the officials, engaged with you?

And I've got one further question after that. Pania will lead that one. Yeah. Hi, kia ora. Thank you for your question. Um, yes, that's correct. We have engaged with officials from Ministry for the Environment. That has been in particular with the post-settlement governance entities.

Uh, we've been engaging for, quite some time, but obviously we didn't know the detail of what was proposed until just prior to Christmas. Okay. However, we had been anticipating something similar being, presented through these bills. So we have worked closely with MFE officials to work through what could the RPC look like under the new, legislation, and hence why our submissions around how the Regional Planning Committee could be adjusted, to include territorial authorities and look carefully at the

roles that are coming through in these new bills, like with regional spatial plans, and how that is an appropriate, work stream for the Regional Planning Committee. Uh, we've also briefly discussed what might come through in terms of the combined territorial-- combined territory board, sorry I forget the exact name, under the simplifying local government. Um, similar, similar, solutions could be put forward there.

So we, we cannot see why this cannot be dealt with promptly prior to the, bills being enacted. Yeah, just suppl- supplementing on that- Yeah, go for a sup-- yeah. Thank you. Um, because that, that's what I'm thinking about, like 'cause you've provided practical, recommendations for the protection of what you currently do, but also offerings as to how to strengthen it.

Um, and that's come through quite strongly across iwi, where they're like, "Well, we've negotiated a treaty settlement. We've got these provisions here." Maybe it's a schedule in the legislation that specifies that at least your roles, and functions are protected or noted as a special feature of your district.

Would something of that nature, provide a peace of mind both to the iwi and councils, that you will still be able to carry out these important functions for your district? I th- I think those are, um... That is one possible solution. The Regional Planning Committee has its own specific legislation. That's another way, that that could be dealt with, through amending that legislation. 'Cause we're obviously not mentioned in the RMA currently. The way it was done was through this bespoke piece of legislation. I think it's important to note though that there are two

fundamental aspects to honoring treaty settlements. The first is structural for us, which is the RPC is a framework, is a structure, a governance committee that would need to be continued, but the second one is substantive, and that goes to the heart of other people's, submissions as well as our own around those, Māori interests provisions being retained.

Because the Regional Planning Committee gives effect to the desire of hapū and iwi to be active decision makers around resource management, and unless those provisions are retained in the bills going forward, there will be no substance around Māori interests for us to be governors, making decisions about. So it's twofold, structural and substantive, which goes beyond just a schedule.

Sorry, just to make that point abundantly clear. Look, I might just jump in there kind of a s- off that, because you talk about treaty partnerships working well in Hawke's Bay. Um, and obviously water is a, is a massive issue- Mm... in Hawke's Bay, as we know, with over-allocated aquifer and obviously, you know, a lot of work going on to look at water storage projects.

Mm-hmm. Um, how heavily has the RPC been involved in, those discussions, in that work? Um- Yeah... and how do you see, the current legislation, as it is impacting that? Yeah. Um, thank you, Chair. That's a great question, and I think when we look at, the strong relationships and partnerships across our region, we know that we have many forums where PSGEs are at the leadership table with us together as councils and as regional leaders. And one of those is around water security, which for the

PSGEs is also a priority. So, their partnership at the table is crucial, and they are there, with the right PSGEs, particularly w- around water storage, as you noted, in the Hiritanga Water Storage Scheme. Um, and the Matariki Governance, Group, which exists in our, in our region, which brings together the, the mayors, the, regional council chair, and the PSGEs and Ngāti Kahungunu iwi. So I think it's an example of strong relationships across many forums, including the RPC. Now, I, I think if we, start to weaken those relationships through

legislation or policy, they might not be as strong in these other forums where we're collectively trying to achieve things like water security... housing initiatives, that PSGs are heavily involved in. And then regional s- it makes sense that regional spatial planning is a forum that they sit at, like we do with the current RPC structure.

Um, so I, I f- I guess what I'm saying here is the PSGs are crucial as regional leaders at the same table across all our major projects and regional priorities as well as... And it makes sense that they're there for regional spatial planning when it comes to investment in the future of our region.

Okay. Thank you. Thank you, Hinewai. Uh, Lan has, another question. You know, korua, it's basic- you've basically answered it in terms of clarifying, you know, the ask, but I just wanna just be completely sure. So if, for example, there is an overarching provision for, say, say equivalent to the RMA, like you say, same or better, are you saying as long as the RPC is enabled and you can u- you can see that the powers are still there, then you don't need a specific mention in the bills? Or, or are you kind of

Our first point today is about environmental limits. Regional councils must retain the ability to set and enforce environmental limits that are necessary to protect freshwater ecosystems, community values, and public wellbeing. From a scientific perspective, prevention and protection is far easier than restoration. Freshwater ecosystems do not always respond gradually to pressure.

In many cases, they can absorb change for long periods and then suddenly shift into a degraded state once ecological thresholds are crossed. These are often referred to as ecological tipping points. Once those tipping points are crossed, ecosystems can become much more difficult, time-consuming, and expensive to restore.And we see this clearly in shallow lakes in Aotearoa. Across New Zealand and internationally, shallow lakes that accumulate too many nutrients can shift from clear water systems to turbid systems, and once that shifts occurs,

it can be very difficult, if not possible, to reverse. Te Waihora or Lake Ellesmere is a well-known example. After shifting from a highly valued clear water macrophyte dominated system to a turbid algal dominated one decades ago, management efforts over more than sixty years have not returned this lake to its previous state. Groundwater systems present a related challenge. Because groundwater states, or because groundwater rather, moves slowly through aquifers, contaminants such as nitrate can accumulate gradually over decades.

In parts of Canterbury and Southland, nitrate concentrations in groundwater have increased steadily over several decades. This means the impacts of today's land use decisions may not be full-fully visible for many years and once contamination occurs, it can take a very long time to reverse.

These examples highlight a critical point for policy. Freshwater systems often store and accumulate pressures over time, and once ecological thresholds are crossed, recovery can be extremely difficult. So environmental limits are therefore essential. They help ensure that pressures on freshwater ecosystems do not build to the point where those thresholds are exceeded.

Protecting ecosystem health early is the most reliable way to ensure that freshwater contamination, or rather freshwater, continues to sustain communities, economies, and ecosystems over the long term, and also protect future generations from costly management and mitigation actions. We note district councils such as Selwyn and Angor are already looking at expensive water treatment programs to address nitrate pollution in their groundwater sources.

For that reason, environmental limits should remain a firm and enforceable foundation of the planning system. Our second point concerns what national monitoring's been, data's tell-tells us about freshwater trends and the importance of recognizing cumulative effects. The Ministry for the Environment's National and, Environmental Reporting shows that many indicators of freshwater ecosystem health remain under pressure.

For example, around fifty-five percent of New Zealand's river length shows ecological conditions consistent with moderate or severe nutrient enrichment based on national macroinvertebrate monitoring. Trends have been worsening at more than half of monitored sites over the past two decades. Nearly half of New Zealand's lakes show signs of poor or very poor health in terms of nutrient enrichment, with conditions worsening over time at forty-five percent of sites. And forty-one percent of groundwater monitoring sites show nitrate levels indicating

accumulation of excess nitrate from human activities, with concentrations increasing at nearly half of sites. These patterns have been observed consistently over many years. The important point is that freshwater ecosystems respond to the cumulative effects of many decisions, including land use change, water allocation, nutrient inputs, sediment, and habitat modifications. Individually, many decisions appear to have only minor effects at the site scale.

However, when repeated across catchments and over time, their combined impacts can become significant. So to reiterate, many of the pressures on freshwater arise not from one big decision, but from the cumulative effect of many small ones. The national data reflects the accumulative effect of these pressures. And because these changes occur gradually, degradation may not become obvious until ecological thresholds are crossed. This is why the planning system needs to set clear environmental objectives, establish limits that

reflect ecological thresholds, explicitly recognize and manage cumulative effects, and support long-term monitoring and accountability. Without these elements, it becomes very difficult to know whether policies are achieving environmental outcomes.

Each reform process involves extensive scientific input, modeling, consultation, and planning at both national and regional levels. improvements can occur. Despite sustained effort, policy changes at both national and regional levels have repeatedly interrupted progress. As a result, long-standing allocation issues remain unresolved. Consented water abstraction can still exceed summer low flows, and some abstractions are not subject to minimum flow provisions under the existing regional plan dest- despite permits expiring under the RMA in twenty twenty-oneResolving these challenges requires careful planning, robust science, and long-term collaboration between communities, iwi, and water users.

Frequent policy change also creates incentives for parties to delay planning processes in anticipation of more favorable future rules. From a f- scientific perspective, environmental management works best when policies are clear, durable, and supported across political cycles.

Long-term environmental problems require stable long-term policy solutions. We would like this committee to encourage the government to carefully consider the costs and risks of frequent policy change, and to avoid policy settings that allow environmental limits to be overridden.

In our experience, such approaches ultimately prove counterproductive and very costly. So in closing, we would like to emphasize that healthy freshwater systems are not just an environmental issue, they are the natural infrastructure that is fundamental to the wellbeing of our communities and economies.

Improving freshwater outcomes requires clear environmental limits, strong monitoring, and stable policy settings. Ensuring that environmental management remains grounded in robust science, mātauranga Māori, and the principles of Te Tiriti o Waitangi will help deliver better outcomes for both current and future generations.
